original_abstract:
In a remarkably short span of time, GCC governments have successfully established comprehensive national systems of public higher education alongside a diverse array of private institutions. This expansion has significantly enhanced accessibility, proving particularly transformative for women; students who were previously restricted by cultural hesitations regarding international travel can now pursue advanced degrees closer to home. Furthermore, the region’s development as an educational hub means that the children of expatriate workers, regardless of gender, are no longer forced to leave the Gulf to obtain a college education.

However, these rapid "educational revolutions" face complex challenges regarding long-term viability and cultural alignment. While the introduction of private competition has spurred accountability and quality assurance reforms in public universities, research output remains modest. More critically, the heavy reliance on transplanted Western institutional models and English-medium instruction creates a tension between globalization and the preservation of Arab-Islamic identity. This paper argues that for these reforms to achieve their overarching goals of modernization and workforce nationalization, they must be integrated with broader socio-political and economic shifts. Education cannot function in a vacuum; it requires a cohesive policy environment—addressing labor market incentives and gender-based participation barriers—to truly fulfill its promise of social and cultural development.
humanized_abstract:
The GCC countries have, remarkably quickly, developed a system of public higher education at the national level, as well as an impressive variety of private sector colleges and universities. The result has been that more students than ever before are able to enter higher education. Women, in particular, have benefited from the new system, as cultural reservations about sending women to study abroad have been easier to overcome in the Gulf than in other parts of the developing world. Expatriate students, men and women alike, are no longer forced to leave the Gulf to complete their tertiary education.

The speed with which whole systems change can be breathtaking but their sustainability and compatibility with existing social and cultural norms is still a highly contested issue. The reforms in the public higher education sector prompted by the private sector's increased involvement appear to have a tenuous impact on research output. Moreover, the imposition of a new layer of internationally benchmarked pedagogical and academic practices alongside the adoption of English as a language of instruction has arguably undermined the globalization of higher education while at the same time creating a major crisis of identity relating to Arab-Islamic culture. In order to realize the aims of the modernization strategy and to facilitate the (re-)employment of graduates and thus to bring about a better social and cultural development of the society in question, a number of other policy measures have to be in place. Among these are, for example, measures designed to correct labor market incentives, and to raise the participation of women in higher education which is currently low.
